references.notes: The term 'محرم' (maḥram) refers to a sacred, inviolable precinct, indicating a pre-Islamic concept of sacred geography where certain actions were forbidden.
The poet swears an oath by 'the House' (al-Bayt, referring to the Kaaba) and explicitly mentions the ritual of circumambulation (ṭawāf), anchoring the oath to a sacred entity and practice.
The verse refers to 'Allah' as an omniscient deity who knows what is hidden in people's souls, reflecting a pre-Islamic or early transitional belief in a supreme divine being.
The phrase 'يوم الحساب' (Day of Reckoning) refers to a concept of divine judgment and accountability for deeds, which, while prominent in Islam, had precursors in pre-Islamic Arabian thought.
The term 'منايا' (fates/deaths) is used to describe death as an inevitable and predetermined force, a common pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The term 'المنايا' (fates/deaths) is used to describe death as an indiscriminate and inescapable force, reinforcing a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of fate.
The term 'المنايا' (fates/deaths) is used to emphasize the inescapability of fate, even through extreme measures, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological belief.
This verse describes a gazelle passing from right to left ('sanḥan'), which was considered a good omen in pre-Islamic Arabian augury.
This verse lists 'yamīn' (oath) as one of the three ways to resolve a right, highlighting its significance as a binding legal and religious practice in pre-Islamic society.
This verse mentions 'al-tilāʾ,' which refers to the act of swearing an oath, emphasizing its role in establishing guarantees and justice in pre-Islamic tribal law.
This verse refers to a 'muqasama,' a solemn oath, often involving ritualistic elements like blood, used to bind parties in pre-Islamic tribal agreements.
This verse mentions 'hadyan,' referring to an animal offering or sacrifice, a common ritual practice in pre-Islamic Arabian religion, often associated with sanctuaries.
The phrase 'تالله' is an explicit oath by God, a common pre-Islamic practice invoking the supreme deity.
The term 'الدهر' (dahr) is used here to denote fate or time as a powerful, often destructive, cosmological force, a key pre-Islamic concept.
The phrase 'غير ملعن القدر' refers to not being cursed by 'القدر' (qadar), which signifies fate or destiny as a pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The poet swears an oath by the sacred stations of Minā, a significant site in pre-Islamic pilgrimage rituals.
This verse explicitly describes a pre-Islamic ritual sacrifice (النسك, العتر) performed at a standing stone or altar (منصب), indicating a sacred space and ritual act.
The phrase "لعمر الله" is a common pre-Islamic oath formula, invoking a supreme deity.
The phrase "أمضي الفؤولا" (I proceed with the omens) explicitly refers to the pre-Islamic practice of taking and following omens (fa'l) before undertaking an action, which is a form of divination.
This verse contains a pre-Islamic oath formula invoking the life of the father, which was a common and significant form of oath in that period.
This verse describes the pre-Islamic ritual of slaughtering camels (ʿaqr) and casting lots (maysir) for their meat, a practice associated with generosity and tribal honor.
The term 'أغوال' (ghouls) explicitly refers to a type of jinn or malevolent supernatural entity in pre-Islamic Arabian folklore.

This verse refers to the belief in 'inauspicious hours' (ساعة نحس) to be avoided and 'auspicious hours' (بأسعد), reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of time's inherent qualities and its influence on human affairs.

The phrase 'at the ḥujurāt, the fire of the kindled' suggests a communal gathering for a ritual around a fire in designated sacred enclosures, fitting the description of pre-Islamic ritual spaces and practices.
The verse refers to 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as an enduring, non-perishing entity, consistent with its pre-Islamic conceptualization as a powerful cosmological force.
This verse again mentions 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as an active agent that can be 'astray' or misleading, reinforcing its role as a cosmological force in pre-Islamic belief.

This verse refers to the concept of the sacred months (الأشهر الحرم) becoming lawful (أحلت), which regulated activities like travel and warfare in pre-Islamic Arabia, indicating a performative dimension of religious practice.
